title: Weighted Word Mapping
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: Pareto-optimal challenge: the single recommended solution is also the speed extreme and the memory extreme. Per the repo standard (no coincident files), only solution.cpp is shipped; there is no separate solution-runtime or solution-memory file.

variants.languageReason: The algorithm is a lower-bound linear scan over at most 1000 lowercase characters, using direct 26-entry table lookups and a pre-sized output string. C++ matches LeetCode's vector<string>/vector<int> signature, avoids interpreter and managed-runtime overhead, and keeps auxiliary memory constant.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. Native character loops, direct vector/string access, one output allocation, and compact scalar state make it the strongest runtime and memory fit for this weighted scan.

Considered for native execution and manual memory control, but LeetCode's C API would add result allocation and string-array pointer boilerplate without reducing auxiliary memory below the C++ version.
Considered for native performance and safety, but bounds-checking details and platform wrapper overhead do not improve a fixed 26-entry lookup over tiny strings.
Considered for compiled loops and straightforward slices, but runtime metadata and bounds checks are weaker constant factors than C++ for this direct micro-loop.
Considered because managed runtimes handle string scans well, but object/runtime overhead and string-builder-style output are unnecessary for 100 words of length at most 10.
Considered because the constraints are small enough for acceptance, but interpreter/VM overhead per character is not ideal for the top 1% execution-time target.

variants.approachJustification: For each word, sum weights[c - 'a'], reduce the sum modulo 26, and store 'z' - residue in a pre-sized result string. This is both the fastest and leanest exact approach because every input character may affect the output.

reasoningSummary: Each word is independent, lowercase letters form a dense 26-entry address space, and reverse-alphabet mapping is the arithmetic expression 'z' - (sum % 26). The solution scans every character once, performs one modulo per word, and writes directly into the final output string.
